title: CODE OF PRACTICE FOR THE PREVENTION AND REDUCTION OF PATULIN CONTAMINATION IN APPLE  JUICE AND APPLE JUICE INGREDIENTS IN OTHER BEVERAGES

sections.section: CODE OF PRACTICE FOR THE PREVENTION AND REDUCTION OF PATULIN CONTAMINATION IN APPLE  JUICE AND APPLE JUICE INGREDIENTS IN OTHER BEVERAGES
sections.summary: Document title only: Code of Practice for the prevention and reduction of patulin contamination in apple juice and apple juice ingredients in other beverages.

sections.section: CAC/RCP 50-2003
sections.summary: Document code only: CAC/RCP 50-2003.

sections.section: INTRODUCTION
sections.summary: Introduces patulin as a mycotoxin mainly from Penicillium expansum affecting apples and apple products, notes that fermentation largely removes patulin while thermal processing and ascorbic acid have limited/variable effects, summarizes toxicology including JECFA's provisional tolerable intake (0.4 µg/kg bw/day), explains that patulin often arises from mould‑damaged or internally infected fruit and may persist despite washing or removal of visibly mouldy tissue, and states recommendations are divided into GAP and GMP measures.

sections.section: 11.  Apples of poor mineral composition are more likely to suffer physiological disorders in  store  and  hence  are  more  susceptible  to  particular  types  of  rot  especially  by Gloeosporium spp  and  secondary  rots  such  as Penicillium .    Consignments  of apples  for  the  fresh  fruit  market  which  do  not  meet  the  recommended  mineral compositional  standards,  as  determined  by  fruit analysis,  should  therefore  be excluded from long-term storage i.e. storage for longer than 3-4 months.
sections.summary: States that apples with poor mineral composition are more prone to physiological disorders and rot, and consignments not meeting recommended mineral standards should be excluded from long‑term storage (longer than 3–4 months).

sections.section: 12.  Where levels of minerals in the fruit for the fresh fruit market are outside optimum ranges, improving calcium and phosphorus levels in the fruit, particularly increasing the calcium/potassium ratio by controlled fertiliser usage, will improve cell structure, which will then reduce susceptibility to rotting.
sections.summary: Recommends correcting mineral imbalances—especially increasing calcium and phosphorus and the calcium/potassium ratio via controlled fertilizer use—to improve cell structure and reduce susceptibility to rotting.

sections.section: 13.  Records  of  rot  levels  should  be  kept  each  year  for  individual  orchards  since historical data is the best guide, at present, to potential rot levels, which will indicate the  need  for  fungicide  application  and  the  storage  potential  of  the  fruit  from  that orchard.
sections.summary: Requires keeping annual records of rot levels for individual orchards as the best current guide to likely rot, fungicide needs, and storage potential.

sections.section: ## Harvesting and transportation of fruit
sections.summary: Section heading only: Harvesting and transportation of fruit.

sections.section: 14.  Apples  for  processing  are  from  two  different origins:
sections.summary: Introductory heading only indicating apples for processing originate from two different sources.

sections.section: ## a) Mechanically harvested fruit
sections.summary: Defines mechanically harvested fruit (tree shaking and ground collection) and requires gentle handling, removal of deteriorated fallen fruit before shaking, transport to processing within 3 days of harvest, and use of clean, dry, debris‑free containers.

sections.section: 21. Ideally all fruit should be picked in dry weather conditions, when the fruit is mature, and placed in clean bins or other containers (e.g. boxes) suitable for transportation directly to store.
sections.summary: Requires picking in dry conditions when fruit is mature into clean bins or boxes (cleaned by hosing or scrubbing with soap and water), drying containers before use, and avoiding exposure of fruit to rain.

sections.section: 22. Adequate training and supervision should be provided to ensure good damage-free picking practice.
sections.summary: Requires providing adequate training and supervision to ensure damage‑free picking practices.

sections.section: 23. All fruit in which the skin is damaged, or with the flesh exposed, as well as all diseased fruit, should be rejected in the orchard at the time of picking and fruit bruising should be minimised as far as possible.
sections.summary: Requires rejecting in the orchard any fruit with skin damage, exposed flesh, or disease at picking and minimizing bruising.

sections.section: 24. All soil-contaminated fruit, i.e. rain splashed fruit or fruit on the ground, should be rejected for storage purposes.
sections.summary: States that soil‑contaminated fruit (rain‑splashed or fruit from the ground) must be rejected for storage.

sections.section: 25. Care must be taken to avoid the inclusion of leaves, twigs etc. in the picked fruit.
sections.summary: Requires avoiding inclusion of leaves, twigs, and similar foreign material in picked fruit.

sections.section: 26. Fruit should be placed in cold storage within 18 hours of harvest and cooled to the recommended temperatures (see Table 1) within 3-4 days of picking.
sections.summary: Requires placing fruit into cold storage within 18 hours of harvest and cooling to recommended temperatures within 3–4 days of picking.

sections.section: 27. During transport and storage, measures should be taken to avoid soil contamination.
sections.summary: Requires taking measures during transport and storage to prevent soil contamination of fruit.

sections.section: 28. Care must be taken during handling and transport of the bins or boxes in the orchard, and between the orchard and store, to avoid soil contamination of the container and the fruit and to minimize physical damage e.g. bruising of the fruit.
sections.summary: Requires careful handling and transport of bins/boxes in the orchard and between orchard and store to avoid soil contamination of containers and fruit and to minimize physical damage such as bruising.

sections.section: 29. Harvested fruit should not be left in the orchard overnight but moved to a hard standing area, preferably under cover.
sections.summary: Mandates that harvested fruit not be left overnight in the orchard but moved to a hard standing area, preferably under cover.

sections.section: 30. All fruit, whether for the fresh market or for later processing, should be handled as gently as possible and every effort made to minimise physical damage e.g. bruising at all stages of post-harvest handling prior to pressing.
sections.summary: Requires that all fruit (for fresh market or processing) be handled as gently as possible at all post‑harvest stages prior to pressing to minimize physical damage such as bruising.

sections.section: 31. Apple growers, and other producers of juice who do not have controlled storage facilities, need to ensure that fruits for juicing are pressed as soon as possible after picking.
sections.summary: Advises apple growers and juice producers without controlled storage to press fruits as soon as possible after picking, and for controlled‑atmosphere storage to check gas tightness, test monitoring equipment, and pre‑cool stores; it also permits post‑harvest fungicide use where authorized, requires regular (at least monthly) inspection and record‑keeping for rot levels with appropriate sampling near inspection hatches, and calls for remedial action when samples indicate deterioration, including specified cooling and controlled‑atmosphere timing targets.

sections.section: 37.  Mould growth normally occurs in a warm  environment.  Rapid  cooling  and maintenance of store atmosphere conditions will improve fruit condition. Ideally fruit should  be  loaded  and  cooled  to  less  than  5  °C  in  3-4  days  and  to optimum temperatures within a further 2 days. Controlled atmosphere conditions should be achieved within 7-10 days from the start of loading, and ultra-low oxygen regimes (i.e. less than 1.8% oxygen) should be established within a further 7 days.
sections.summary: Notes mould grows in warm conditions and requires rapid cooling and maintenance of store atmosphere to improve fruit condition, with targets to load and cool fruit to <5 °C within 3–4 days and to optimum temperatures within a further 2 days, achieve controlled atmosphere within 7–10 days, and establish ultra‑low oxygen (<1.8% O2) regimes within an additional ~7 days.

sections.section: Post-storage grading of fruit for the fresh market or juice manufacture
sections.summary: Requires removal of all rotten fruit and storage of wholesome fruit in clean bulk containers; when selecting for retail, containers remaining for juicing must be marked and returned to cold store within 12 hours with time at ambient minimized (preferably <5 °C), and bruising must be minimized because it encourages patulin formation, especially if fruit is held >24 hours at ambient temperature.

sections.section: II. RECOMMENDED PRACTICES BASED ON GMP
sections.summary: Non‑substantive heading introducing recommended practices based on Good Manufacturing Practice (GMP).

sections.section: Transportation, checking, and pressing of fruit
sections.summary: Non‑substantive heading for recommendations on transportation, checking, and pressing of fruit.

sections.section: Mechanically harvested fruit and fruit for the fresh market
sections.summary: Non‑substantive heading for guidance specific to mechanically harvested fruit and fruit for the fresh market (includes subheading for fruit for the fresh market).

sections.section: 41.  Stored  fruit  should  be  transported  from  the  cold  store  to  the  processor  in  the shortest time possible (ideally <24 hours to pressing unless cold stored).
sections.summary: Recommends that stored fruit be transported from cold store to the processor in the shortest time possible, ideally less than 24 hours to pressing unless the fruit remains cold‑stored.

sections.section: 42. Adequate training and supervision should be provided to ensure good damage-free picking practice.
sections.summary: States that varieties with an open calyx are prone to core rots and must be checked for internal rots immediately before pressing by random sampling (cut across the equator); if core rot frequency exceeds an agreed maximum the consignment must not be used for juicing and should be rejected if supplier or processor limits are exceeded.

sections.section: 43.  On  arrival  at  the  factory  the  fruit  should  be  checked  for  quality,  particularly  for evidence of both external and internal mould damage (see para. 44).
sections.summary: Requires that fruit be checked on arrival at the factory for quality, with particular attention to evidence of external and internal mould damage.

sections.section: 44.  During  processing  and  prior  to  pressing,  the  fruit  should  be  sorted  carefully  to remove any visually mouldy fruit (check randomly and routinely for internal mould by cutting some fruit as indicated in para. 42) and washed thoroughly, using potable or suitably treated water.
sections.summary: Requires careful sorting during processing to remove visually mouldy fruit, routine internal mould checks (as per para. 42), and thorough washing with potable or suitably treated water, plus cleaning and sanitising of presses and equipment per industry best practice; after pressing, mandates representative sampling and accredited laboratory analysis for patulin, chilling of juice to <5 °C until further processing, and positive release for packing only after patulin is confirmed below the agreed limit with purchase specifications including a patulin limit.

sections.section: 45.  Juice presses and other manufacturing equipment should be cleaned and sanitised in accordance with industry "best practices". Juice presses and other equipment will generally be washed down with pressured water hoses and sanitised by application of a suitable sanitiser, followed by a further rinse with potable cold water. In some
sections.summary: Advises cleaning and sanitising juice presses and equipment according to best practices (typically once per shift or day in continuous plants), requires post‑press sampling and accredited patulin analysis, recommends chilling juice to <5 °C until concentration/packaging/pasteurisation, and permits packing only after patulin results confirm levels below the agreed specification.

sections.section: ## Packaging and final processing of juice
sections.summary: Advises preventing development of moulds (which can produce patulin) during transport and storage, recommends holding juice at ≤5 °C if stored prior to use, and notes that common heat processing destroys fungal spores and vegetative mycelium but will not destroy any patulin already present.

sections.section: ## Quality assessment of juice
sections.summary: Requires purchase specifications for apple juice/concentrates to include a maximum patulin limit based on appropriate analysis, mandates a sampling plan for random product testing, requires packers to verify supplier control, and calls for assessment of quality (°Brix, acidity, flavour, colour, turbidity and microbiological monitoring) plus checks on packaged product; the section also provides a table of recommended air storage temperatures for specific apple varieties.
